(While in position, keep your back leg perpendicular to the ground by sinking your knee downward in a straight line without pushing your hips forward.
Pay attention to your front knee so that it does not move forward past your toes, by keeping your shin vertical/perpendicular to the ground.
If you notice your knee on the front leg moving beyond your toes as you drop down after making adjustments, you may need to move the front foot slightly forward.)

recommendations.title: Align hip and knee joint:make sure your knee and hip are in one line
recommendations.description: Align hip and knee joint:make sure your knee and hip are in one line (your knee is not pointed outward or inward). . recommendations.title: Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly
recommendations.description: Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly (On your stable leg, sit your butt backwards by hinging at your hips and leaning your chest forward slightly as you are lunging.)

recommendations.title: Reduce ROM: If you have tried aigning your hip and knee joint and still have discomfort, decrease how far you lunge
recommendations.description: Reduce ROM: If you have tried aigning your hip and knee joint and still have discomfort, decrease how far you lunge. (If your alignment is perfect but you still feel pain or discomfort, don't go as low and perform the movement in a pain free ROM.)

recommendations.title: High impact: adjusting running surfaces
recommendations.description: High impact: adjusting running surfaces (If your knees feel rattled or unstable, try moving off the concrete. Run on softer surfaces like grass, a flat dirt trail, or a rubberized track to reduce the force hitting your joints.)

recommendations.title: Over striding/under strdiing: every time your foot hits the ground make sure your shin is perpendicular to the ground...
recommendations.description: Over striding/under strdiing: every time your foot hits the ground make sure your shin is perpendicular to the ground, and focus on landing your foot closer to your body right under your hip/where your foot lands should be right below your center of mass. (During the moment your foot hits the ground, make sure your shin is perpendicular/vertical to the ground by landing your foot right under your center of mass or directly under your hip.)

recommendations.title: Quad dominance: if you find you're leaning back while running, bring your trunk slightly forward and maintain with ea...
recommendations.description: Quad dominance: if you find you're leaning back while running, bring your trunk slightly forward and maintain with each stride. (While running, if you notice you tend to lean back, slightly bring your trunk forward by hinging at your hips and shifting your weight forward.)

recommendations.title: Bounce
recommendations.description: Bounce (overall form): once you've leaned forward, incerased your cadence to correct your stride - when landing on the ball of your foot focus on puhsing the ground backward (instead of pushing up) - tightening your core and squeezing your glute to push can help with this. (If you notice you tend to “bounce” or move up and down a lot while running, increase your cadence (slightly shorten your stride length and increase the frequency of your steps) by making sure you’ve shifted your weight forward and push the ground backwards as you push off.)

recommendations.title: Flat feet - insole with medial arch support or chane in shoes that have incerase medial arch support. Supincated feet...
recommendations.description:
Flat feet - insole with medial arch support or chane in shoes that have incerase medial arch support. Supincated feet - lateral foot support recommended. (If you have flat or notice your arch collapses when your foot hits the ground, focus on equally distributing your weight more towards the middle of your foot (try to equally distribute your eight throughout the entire foot)
After making the corrections/modifications and you are having pain or discomfort, you may need to try an insole with medial arch support to prevent your arch from collapsing and/or your knee from caving in.)

recommendations.title: Supinated feet
recommendations.description:
Supinated feet (If you notice you land with your weight on the outside of your foot, focus on shifting your weight more towards the middle of your foot to equally distribute your weight. 
After making the previous modifications and you're still having pain or discomfort, you may need to look for shoes or insoles that have lateral foot support to help with your foot positioning while running.)

dx_running_uphill_patella.label: Running (uphill) - focus on form instead of pain location
dx_running_uphill_patella.summary: Assessment for Running (uphill) with patella pain
dx_running_uphill_patella.explanation: heel or midfoot strike, dynamic valgus, increased "bounce," anterior knee translation, quad over activation

recommendations.title: Over striding
recommendations.description:
Over striding (probably has heel strike as well): Lean slightly forward and try taking quicker, shorter steps—aim to move your feet at a slightly quicker pace than usual, make sure you land on the balls of your feet to push through the stride, when you push through think about squeezing your glutes. This helps reduce how much impact and force goes through your knees with each step, which can ease pain, especially going uphill. Think of it like turning a bike pedal faster with less push—it’s smoother and gentler on your joints.
(Lean your trunk and shift your weight slightly forward into the hill and take shorter, faster steps. 
Focus on landing on the balls of your feet with each stride. Think about pushing yourself up the hill by giving your glutes a firm squeeze to push the ground backwards. While doing this and you notice your knees tend to collapse inward, think about pushing your knees outward against an invisible wall to help maintain alignment. )

dx_running_downhill_patella.label: Running (downhill) - focus on form instead of pain location
dx_running_downhill_patella.summary: Assessment for Running (downhill) with patella pain

recommendations.title: When running downhill, try taking shorter, quicker steps
recommendations.description:
When running downhill, try taking shorter, quicker steps (and slightly decerase your speed)—almost like you're gently tapping the ground with each foot (land on the balls of your feet). Do this while pushing your hips back and keeping your chest upright. This helps you stay more upright and in control, instead of pounding down hard on your knees with each stride. By keeping your steps light and quick, you reduce the impact on your knees and avoid overbending them with every step. Make sure you are landing on the ball sof your feet and keeping your shins vertical. (Slow down and take shorter, quicker steps. Aim for light, gentle foot landings rather than landing heavily on your feet. 
If you notice you're landing on your heels, focus on landing on the balls of your feet, and be sure to keep your shin vertical/upright. 
If you notice you are leaning forward or feeling uncontrolled, slightly push your hips back while keeping your chest tall and upright. )

recommendations.title: Foot posture: most people need support in there shoe if they have anatomical flat feet ot dynamic pronation
recommendations.description:
Foot posture: most people need support in there shoe if they have anatomical flat feet ot dynamic pronation (If your feet tend to roll inward or your arches look flat, your knee often follows that inward curve. Adding a supportive insole or wearing "stability" shoes can help keep your foundation level and your knees aligned.
If you have flat or notice your arch collapses when your foot hits the ground, focus on equally distributing your weight more towards the middle of your foot (try to equally distribute your eight throughout the entire foot)
After making the corrections/modifications and you are having pain or discomfort, you may need to try an insole with medial arch support to prevent your arch from collapsing and/or your knee from caving in.)

When hiking uphill, try taking shorter steps instead of big ones so you don’t have to bend your knees as sharply. 
(Lean your trunk and shift your weight slightly forward into the hill and take shorter, faster steps. 
Focus on landing on the balls of your feet with each stride. Think about pushing yourself up the hill by giving your glutes a firm squeeze to push the ground backwards to push yourself forward.)

(Lean your chest slightly forward. Land each step on the ball or middle of your foot and squeeze your glute (butt muscle) to drive yourself up the hill. )

recommendations.title: Instruct to take seated breaks before onset of pain. Increase glute activation: slghtly lean forward and squeeze your...
recommendations.description: Instruct to take seated breaks before onset of pain. Increase glute activation: slghtly lean forward and squeeze your glutes with each step pushing up the hill. (If you have chronic pain that is aggravated with prolonged walking, don't wait for your knees to start "talking" to you. If possible, take a quick seated break before you feel any discomfort. This helps manage joint fatigue and keeps you moving longer.)

recommendations.title: equipment

recommendations.description: Try standing with your feet a bit wider than your hips and pointing your toes slightly outwards, and as you go down, push your hips back like you're reaching your butt toward a low chair. Keep your chest up and let your knees stay slightly behind your toes while slightly pushing them outwards to avoid inward collapse. This shift helps take the strain off your knees by getting your hips and thighs to do more of the work. It often feels more stable and comfortable, especially if deep bending bothers your knees. .

As you squat, keep your knees aimed perfectly over your second and third toes, and pointed slightly outwards. Don't let your knees wobble inward or flare out too far. )

recommendations.title: Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly. Weight adjustment: decras...
recommendations.description: Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly. Weight adjustment: decrase weight and readjust. *always cue for pelvic tilt/core engagement* (While squatting if you notice your trunk is too upright, lean your chest slightly more forward by increasing the hinge at your hips. As you stand up, drive through your heels to power the lift.)

recommendations.title: Reduce ROM: If you have tried aligning your hip and knee joint and still have discomfort, decrease how far you squat ...
recommendations.description: Reduce ROM: If you have tried aligning your hip and knee joint and still have discomfort, decrease how far you squat down, pain free range of motion. (After making the previous modifications and you are still having pain or discomfort, perform the squat in a pain free range of motion.)

dx_squatting_barbell_anteromedial_femora.label: Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) - Medial knee pain (loaded LE)
dx_squatting_barbell_anteromedial_femora.summary: Assessment for Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) with anteromedial femoral condyle pain

recommendations.title: Dynamic valgus: slightly turn your toes and knee outward and pretend you're pushing your knee against a wall througho...
recommendations.description: Dynamic valgus: slightly turn your toes and knee outward and pretend you're pushing your knee against a wall throughout the motion. *always cue for pelvic tilt/core engagement* Weight adjustment: decrase weight and readjust. (Try standing with your feet a bit wider than your hips and pointing your toes slightly outwards, and as you go down, push your hips back like you're reaching your butt toward a low chair, and pretend you're pushing your knees against a wall. Keep your chest up and let your knees stay slightly behind your toes. )

recommendations.title: Anterior knee translation
recommendations.description: Anterior knee translation: (As you start to lower the weight, sink your butt directly backward as if reaching for a chair. Keep your shins as vertical as possible by not letting them move forward past your toes.)

dx_squatting_barbell_posteromedial_femor.label: Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) - Posterior knee pain (loaded LE)
dx_squatting_barbell_posteromedial_femor.summary: Assessment for Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) with posteromedial femoral condyle pain
dx_squatting_barbell_posteromedial_femor.explanation: Hamstrings, gastroc, popliteus, meniscus; increased posterior chain activation

recommendations.title: Decrease posterior chain activatoin: if you find you are leaning more or too far forward, adjust your trunk so that y...
recommendations.description: Decrease posterior chain activatoin: if you find you are leaning more or too far forward, adjust your trunk so that your torso is more upright (but not fully straight). (If you find your chest is pointing toward the floor as you squat, lift your torso to a more upright position. You don't need to be perfectly vertical, but keep your chest tall. Tuck your pelvis and engage your core to stay stable and balanced under the weight.)

recommendations.title: Narrow stance
recommendations.description: Narrow stance (quad focused squat): make sure your feet are no wider than the width of your hips, or can be slighlty more inward. Weight adjustment: decrase weight and readjust. *always cue for pelvic tilt/core engagement* (If you’re noticing discomfort in the back of your knee, place your feet in a narrower stance (slightly narrower than hip width) to complete the squat.)

recommendations.title: Deccreases ROM: perform the movement in a pain free range
recommendations.description: Deccreases ROM: perform the movement in a pain free range. (After making the previous modifications and you are still having pain or discomfort, perform the squat in a pain free range of motion.)

dx_squatting_barbell_patella.label: Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) - Central/deep pain (joint)
dx_squatting_barbell_patella.summary: Assessment for Squatting (heavy weight, barbell) with patellofemoral joint (patella) pain

recommendations.title: Overall alignment: make sure your feet are hip width appart or slighlty wider, point your toes slightly outward, tuck...
recommendations.description: Overall alignment: make sure your feet are hip width appart or slighlty wider, point your toes slightly outward, tuck your peliv to engae your core, sit your butt backwards to decease your knees moving forward, push through your heels to return to standing. (While in starting position, make sure your feet are hip width apart, or slightly wider, and slightly turn your toes outward to about 20-30 degrees. When squatting down, keep your knees pointed out like you’re pushing them against a wall, and make sure your chest/trunk remains upright.)

recommendations.title: Weight adjustment: decrase weight and readjust. *always cue for pelvic tilt/core engagement*
recommendations.description: Weight adjustment: decrase weight and readjust. *always cue for pelvic tilt/core engagement* (After making the previous modifications and you still have pain or discomfort, decrease the amount of weight you’re squatting with and see if that alleviates the pain.)

recommendations.title: ROM: If you've already corrected your alignmens, perform te exercise in a pain free range of motion
recommendations.description: ROM: If you've already corrected your alignmens, perform te exercise in a pain free range of motion. (After making the previous modifications and you are still having pain or discomfort, perform the squat in a pain free range of motion.)

dx_stairs_up_patella.label: Ascending stairs - focus on addressing form, will be similar to hiking uphill
dx_stairs_up_patella.summary: Assessment for Ascending stairs with patella pain
dx_stairs_up_patella.explanation: dynamic valgus and/or anterior knee translation, over activation of quads, patellar tracking

recommendations.title: Increase glute activation: slghtly lean forward, push your butt slightly backwards and squeeze your glutes as
recommendations.description: Increase glute activation: slghtly lean forward, push your butt slightly backwards and squeeze your glutes as (and push through your heel) you push your self up after each step. (Lean your chest forward slightly and push your hips back as you step up. Drive firmly through your heel and squeeze your glute (butt muscle) to lift yourself.)

recommendations.title: UE support: if there is a railing nearby, use it to help decrease pressure on your knees
recommendations.description: UE support: if there is a railing nearby, use it to help decrease pressure on your knees. (If there is a railing, use it to place some weight through your arm(s) to help offload your knees.)

recommendations.title: Lateral apporach: if pain still persits, go up the steps side ways which decreases presuure on your knees
recommendations.description: Lateral apporach: if pain still persits, go up the steps side ways which decreases presuure on your knees. (If facing forward still feels uncomfortable, try turning sideways and use the side step technique. If a railing is available, use that to help with your balance. )

recommendations.title: If you notice your knees collapsing inward, focus on pointing your knees outward and pretend you’re pushing them agai...
recommendations.description: If you notice your knees collapsing inward, focus on pointing your knees outward and pretend you’re pushing them against an imaginary wall.

recommendations.title: If your knee pain is more prominent in one knee, use the rule “up with the good,” by leading with the leg that has le...
recommendations.description: If your knee pain is more prominent in one knee, use the rule “up with the good,” by leading with the leg that has less pain while ascending stairs.

recommendations.title: If you’ve tried the previous modifications and still have some discomfort, try turning your body sideways and use the...
recommendations.description: If you’ve tried the previous modifications and still have some discomfort, try turning your body sideways and use the side step technique. If a railing is available, use that to help with your balance.

dx_stairs_down_patella.label: Descending stairs - focus on addressing form instead of pain location (most of the time will be anterio/anteromedial)
dx_stairs_down_patella.summary: Assessment for Descending stairs with patella pain
dx_stairs_down_patella.explanation: PF impairments, OA, valgus, anterio knee translation

recommendations.title: Anterior knee translation/decrease quag activation: push your hips back and slightly lean forward while descenind the...
recommendations.description: Anterior knee translation/decrease quag activation: push your hips back and slightly lean forward while descenind the steps. Make sure you're landing your foot softly, as if you were tapping it on the step, instead of slamming it down quickly. (Push your hips back slightly and lean your chest forward to stay balanced to help with keeping your shins as vertical as possible. Try not to let your knees move forward past your toes. As you reach for the next step, imagine you are gently tapping the surface with your foot rather than slamming it down.)

recommendations.title: UE support: If necessary, use the railing to help decrease pressure on your knees
recommendations.description: UE support: If necessary, use the railing to help decrease pressure on your knees. (If you’ve tried the previous modifications and still have some pain or discomfort, try using a railing if one is available to help take some weight off of your knees.)

recommendations.title: Lateral appraoch: if the above does not help, turn sideways and side step to help deceased pressure on your knees
recommendations.description: Lateral appraoch: if the above does not help, turn sideways and side step to help deceased pressure on your knees. (If you’ve tried the previous modifications and still have some discomfort, try turning your body sideways and use the side step technique. If a railing is available, use that to help with your balance.)

recommendations.title: If you notice your knee is collapsing inward as you land your foot on each step, try pointing your toes and knees sli...
recommendations.description: If you notice your knee is collapsing inward as you land your foot on each step, try pointing your toes and knees slightly outward and imagine you're pushing your knee against an imaginary wall throughout the entire movement.

recommendations.title: Down with the bad: If one knee tends to be more painful than the other, when descending stairs use the “down with the...
recommendations.description: Down with the bad: If one knee tends to be more painful than the other, when descending stairs use the “down with the bad rule.” When going down stairs, lead with the knee that is more painful.

recommendations.title: overall control: If you notice you have heavy foot steps and are slapping your foot on the step, try to make your des...
recommendations.description: overall control: If you notice you have heavy foot steps and are slapping your foot on the step, try to make your descent as silent as possible. Try to gently land your foot with each step.

dx_half_kneeling_patella.label: Half kneeling (focus on overall positioning/comfort) - pain in WB knee
dx_half_kneeling_patella.summary: Assessment for Half kneeling (focus on overall positioning/comfort) with patella pain

recommendations.title: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knee to provide imorpved comfort
recommendations.description: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knee to provide imorpved comfort (If you're having discomfort with the pressure on the front of your knee contacting the surface, place a rolled towel, pillow or other soft time underneath your knee to provide cushioning for improved comfort. )

recommendations.title: Make sure your hip is directly over your bottom knee, and your front shin is vertical like a pillar. Think about keep...
recommendations.description: Make sure your hip is directly over your bottom knee, and your front shin is vertical like a pillar. Think about keeping your legs in a 90-90 position while half kneeling.

recommendations.title: Toe/ankle positoin: If your knee feels unstable, try tucking your toes under so the balls of your feet touch the grou...
recommendations.description:
Toe/ankle positoin: If your knee feels unstable, try tucking your toes under so the balls of your feet touch the ground which provides stability by shifting weight off of your knee onto your foot.
If tucking your toes is uncomfortable, place a rolled up towel right under the ankle joint.

recommendations.title: Weight shift: If the pain persists and you notice you’re leaned back, shift a little more of your weight onto your fr...
recommendations.description: Weight shift: If the pain persists and you notice you’re leaned back, shift a little more of your weight onto your front foot by bringing your body forward.

recommendations.title: If you've tried the previous modifications and are still having some pain or discomfort, try using hand support by gr...
recommendations.description: If you've tried the previous modifications and are still having some pain or discomfort, try using hand support by grabbing onto a sturdy surface like a chair, or the wall for balance and support.

dx_half_kneeling_patella_2.label: Half kneeling (focus on overall positioning/comfort) - pain in non-WB knee
dx_half_kneeling_patella_2.summary: Assessment for Half kneeling (focus on overall positioning/comfort) with patella pain

recommendations.title: Make sure your hip and knee are in a stright line, and your shin is perpendicular tot he gorund
recommendations.description: Make sure your hip and knee are in a stright line, and your shin is perpendicular tot he gorund (Make sure your hip is directly over your bottom knee, and your front shin is vertical like a pillar. Think about keeping your legs in a 90-90 position while half kneeling.)

dx_tall_kneeling_anteromedial_femoral_co.label: Tall kneeling - pain in B knees from WB
dx_tall_kneeling_anteromedial_femoral_co.summary: Assessment for Tall kneeling with anteromedial femoral condyle pain

recommendations.title: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knees to provide imorpved comfort
recommendations.description: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knees to provide imorpved comfort (If you're having discomfort with the pressure on the front of your knees contacting the surface, place a rolled towel, pillow or other soft time underneath your knees to provide cushioning for improved comfort.)

recommendations.title: Jiont alignement
recommendations.description: Jiont alignement: (Make sure your hips are directly over your knees by creating a straight line between the joints and trunk, and your front shins are vertical/perpendicular to the ground. Think about keeping your legs in a 90 degree position.)

recommendations.title: Toe/ankle positoining
recommendations.description:
Toe/ankle positoining: (If your knees feel unstable or you feel too much pressure, try tucking your toes under so the balls of your feet touch the ground which provides stability by shifting weight off of your knee onto your foot
If tucking your toes is uncomfortable, place a rolled up towel right under the ankle joint.)

recommendations.title: After trying the previous modifications and are still having pain or discomfort, try moving your knees slightly wider...
recommendations.description: After trying the previous modifications and are still having pain or discomfort, try moving your knees slightly wider than your hips.

recommendations.title: If you've tried the previous modifications and are still having some pain or discomfort, try using hand support by gr...
recommendations.description: If you've tried the previous modifications and are still having some pain or discomfort, try using hand support by grabbing onto a sturdy surface like a chair, or the wall for balance and support.

dx_full_kneeling_anterolateral_femoral_c.label: Full kneeling - focus on overall positioning/comfort
dx_full_kneeling_anterolateral_femoral_c.summary: Assessment for Full kneeling with anterolateral femoral condyle pain
dx_full_kneeling_anterolateral_femoral_c.explanation: excessive flexion, meniscus, OA, patellar tendon impairment

recommendations.title: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knees to provide imorpved comfort. If you're sit...
recommendations.description: Cushioning: place a rolled towel or another soft item underneath the knees to provide imorpved comfort. If you're sitting on the underside of your feet, tuck your toes and sit on your heels to decerase your knee bend. If sitting on your heels hurts, put a rolled-up towel behind your knees. (If you're having discomfort with the pressure on the front of your knees contacting the surface, place a rolled towel, pillow or other soft time underneath your knees to provide cushioning for improved comfort.)

recommendations.title: Posterior knee compression
recommendations.description: Posterior knee compression: (If sitting on your heels feels like a "pinch" in the back of your knee, place a rolled-up towel deep into the crease of your knees before sitting back. This creates a tiny bit of space in the joint and reduces pressure.)

recommendations.title:
If you're sitting on the underside of your feet, tuck your toes and sit on your heels to decrease your knee bend.
If ...
recommendations.description:
If you're sitting on the underside of your feet, tuck your toes and sit on your heels to decrease your knee bend.
If tucking your toes is uncomfortable, place a rolled up towel right under the ankle joint.
If you can't reach your heels without pain, place a yoga block or a thick firm pillow between your feet. Sit your butt onto the block instead of your heels to reduce how sharply your knees have to bend.

dx_bending_down_anteromedial_femoral_con.label: Bending down (focus on teaching bending mechancis as a whole) - Anterior/anteromedial
dx_bending_down_anteromedial_femoral_con.summary: Assessment for Bending down (focus on teaching bending mechancis as a whole) with anteromedial femoral condyle pain

recommendations.title: Instead of bending your knees forward, hinge at your hips by pushing your hips back like you're reaching for a chair ...
recommendations.description:
Instead of bending your knees forward, hinge at your hips by pushing your hips back like you're reaching for a chair to bend down. When returning to standing, think about reversing the motion. If you are bending to lift up a heavy object, keep the object close to your center of gravity. (Try not to bend at your back while keeping your knees straight/locking out your knees
If you’re bending down and notice your knees move forward past your toes, try to hinge at your hips by pushing your hips backwards as if you were reaching for a chair (essentially like performing a squat), and think about keeping your shins as vertical as possible. Keep your chest upright as well. 
When returning to standing, think about reversing the movement.
If you're attempting to lift a heavy object, make sure to keep it closer to your body.)

recommendations.title: If you notice your knees collapsing inward during this movement while in starting position, point your toes outward a...
recommendations.description: If you notice your knees collapsing inward during this movement while in starting position, point your toes outward about 20-30 degrees. While bending, keep your knees pointed outward by imagining you’re pushing them against an imaginary wall to maintain this position.

dx_sitting_down_anterolateral_femoral_co.label: Sitting down (essentially take from squatting) - Anterior/anteromedial
dx_sitting_down_anterolateral_femoral_co.summary: Assessment for Sitting down (essentially take from squatting) with anterolateral femoral condyle pain

recommendations.title: Sitting down: Make sure your feet are hip width apart or slightly wider, hine your hips and sit your butt backwards t...
recommendations.description:
Sitting down: Make sure your feet are hip width apart or slightly wider, hine your hips and sit your butt backwards to maintain a vertical shin position (do this slowly and controlled to avoid plopping - think about keeping your nose over your toes), as you keep pushing your butt back and down keep your knees pointed outward (dont let them collapse inward). Control the descent until you've softly landed in the chair. . (Before sitting, make sure you feet are hip width apart or slightly wider, then hinge at your hips to sit your butt backwards towards the seat in a slow and controlled manner (don't plop down)
While descending think about keeping your nose over your toes to keep your weight balanced
While descending, keep your knees pointed outward throughout the whole movement, don't let them collapse inward)

recommendations.title: UE support: if it is na arm chiar, use the arm rest to help support you
recommendations.description: UE support: if it is na arm chiar, use the arm rest to help support you (if you’re using an arm chair, use one or both armrests to help support you, while keeping the same form as previosuly demsontrted)

recommendations.title: Chair height: low chairs can cause inceased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair
recommendations.description: Chair height: low chairs can cause inceased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair. (low chairs can cause increased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair and maintain the same form modifications previously provided)

dx_standing_up_anteromedial_femoral_cond.label: Standing up - focus on overall neuromusclular coordinatoin rather than pain location
dx_standing_up_anteromedial_femoral_cond.summary: Assessment for Standing up with anteromedial femoral condyle pain
dx_standing_up_anteromedial_femoral_cond.explanation: anterior knee translation, valgus, varus, ITB impairemnt, OA, meniscus

recommendations.title: Sequencing: scoot forward ont he chair towards the edge, make sure your feet are hip widht apart or slightly wider. L...
recommendations.description:
Sequencing: scoot forward ont he chair towards the edge, make sure your feet are hip widht apart or slightly wider. Lean your trunk forward (nose over toes), then push through your heels to stand stright up. (Before you stand up. Scoot your butt towards the edge of the chair
Make sure your feet are hip width apart or slightly wider
Lean your trunk forward (thin about bringing your nose over your toes) by hinging at your hips, do not round your back
Then push the ground away from you to stand straight up
Think about reversing the sitting sequence )

recommendations.title: UE support: if it is na arm chiar, use the arm rest to help support you to push you into standing
recommendations.description: UE support: if it is na arm chiar, use the arm rest to help support you to push you into standing (if you’re using an arm chair, use the arm rest to help support you to push you into standing)

recommendations.title: . Chair height: low chairs can cause inceased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair
recommendations.description: . Chair height: low chairs can cause inceased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair (low chairs can cause increased pressure on your knees, if possible sit in a higher chair next time to make standing up easier. )

dx_rowing_machine_patella.label: Rowing machine - Anterior
dx_rowing_machine_patella.summary: Assessment for Rowing machine with patella pain

recommendations.title: Don't slide the seat all the way forward. Stop when your shins are vertical to avoid deep knee bends
recommendations.description: Don't slide the seat all the way forward. Stop when your shins are vertical to avoid deep knee bends. (Don't slide the seat all the way forward until it hits your heels. Stop when your shins are vertical or perpendicular to the ground. This prevents "over-bending" and keeps the pressure off your kneecaps.)

recommendations.title: Foot placement: If your knees feel "pinched" at the start of the stroke, lower your footplates by one or two notches....
recommendations.description: Foot placement: If your knees feel "pinched" at the start of the stroke, lower your footplates by one or two notches. This creates a more open angle for your knees and makes it easier to sit tall.

recommendations.title: Toe drive vs full foot: As you push away, drive through your entire foot—not just your toes. Imagine "jumping" off th...
recommendations.description: Toe drive vs full foot: As you push away, drive through your entire foot—not just your toes. Imagine "jumping" off the footplates while keeping your core engaged and maintaining upright trunk posture.

recommendations.title: Overall alignment/valgus: Keep your knees aimed directly over your second toes throughout the whole stroke. Don't let...
recommendations.description: Overall alignment/valgus: Keep your knees aimed directly over your second toes throughout the whole stroke. Don't let them collapse inward or fall too far outward as you pull forward or push back.

recommendations.title: Avoid full ext: When your legs are fully extended at the end of the stroke, avoid snapping or locking them straight. ...
recommendations.description: Avoid full ext: When your legs are fully extended at the end of the stroke, avoid snapping or locking them straight. Keep a small bend in your knees to keep the tension in your muscles, not your joints.

dx_jumping_anteromedial_femoral_condyle.label: Jumping - focus on form rather than pain location
dx_jumping_anteromedial_femoral_condyle.summary: Assessment for Jumping with anteromedial femoral condyle pain
dx_jumping_anteromedial_femoral_condyle.explanation: Stiff landing; valgus with concentric and eccentric phase

recommendations.title: Concentric phase form: think about starting to jump as if you're performing a squat
recommendations.description:
Concentric phase form: think about starting to jump as if you're performing a squat (start by sitting your butt backwards to keep your knees from moving forward, leaning yout trunk lsightly forward, and keep your knees pointed outwards if the are collapsing in. (While in the starting position, think about starting to jump as if you're performing a squat. Sit your butt back and lean your trunk forward slightly by hinging at your hips to lower, while making sure your shins stay as vertical as possible and aligned with your 2nd-3rd toes
From this position before you jump, evenly distribute your weight throughout your entire foot, then in one swift motion push the ground away from you to jump.)

recommendations.title: Landing: Land as quietly as possible by returning to the starting position
recommendations.description:
Landing: Land as quietly as possible by returning to the starting position (Bend your hips and knees to absorb the shock like a spring) - dont land with your knees and legs in a stiff position (If you notice you have a hard landing that “rattles” your knees, think about landing as quietly as possible. To do this, as you descend closer to the ground, stick your butt backwards and bring your trunk slightly forward by hinging your hips. 
As you land slightly bend your knees to help absorb the “shock,”don't let your knees move past your toes, and keep your knees aligned with your 2nd-3rd toe. )

dx_pivoting_patella.label: Cutting/pivoting - Medial / Lateral
dx_pivoting_patella.summary: Assessment for Cutting/pivoting with patella pain

recommendations.title: Instead of a sharp "plant and twist," take several small steps to round out your turn
recommendations.description:
Instead of a sharp "plant and twist," take several small steps to round out your turn (If you notice you plant your foot and then make a sharp twist, try taking several small steps to round out your turn to avoid large twisting forces on your knees.
While doing this, sit your butt backward and bring your trunk slightly forward )

dx_prolonged_sitting_patella.label: Prolonged sitting - focus on form and positional breaks
dx_prolonged_sitting_patella.summary: Assessment for Prolonged sitting with patella pain

recommendations.title: Straighten your legs/knees whiel sitting to keep the "oil" moving in your joints. Or take standing breaks beofre onse...
recommendations.description:
Straighten your legs/knees whiel sitting to keep the "oil" moving in your joints. Or take standing breaks beofre onset of pain (If you notice onset of pain or stiffness after a certain amount of time after sitting, try to periodically straighten your legs/knees while sitting BEFORE onset of pain. Do this periodically while sitting if not able to stand.
If possible, take standing breaks periodically BEFORE usual onset of pain.)

recommendations.title: LE positiong/ergonimics: Ideally, your legs should be at a 90 degree angle while sitting with your feet touching flat...
recommendations.description:
LE positiong/ergonimics: Ideally, your legs should be at a 90 degree angle while sitting with your feet touching flat on the ground (or on a foot stool). If you usually are in a lower chair switch to a high chair if possible. Or try sitting on folded blankets or towels
Maybe show ergonomic depiction (especially if a lot of folks are desk workers) )

dx_prolonged_standing_patella.label: Prolonged standing - focus on form and positional breaks
dx_prolonged_standing_patella.summary: Assessment for Prolonged standing with patella pain

recommendations.title: Don't lock your knees straight. Keep a tiny, invisible bend and shift your weight from foot to foot. Take seated brea...
recommendations.description:
Don't lock your knees straight. Keep a tiny, invisible bend and shift your weight from foot to foot. Take seated breaks beofre onset of pain (If you notice your knees are “locked” while standing, try standing with a slight microbend in your knees.
If onset of pain comes after a certain period of time, take seated rest breaks periodically BEFORE onset of pain.
You can also perform light exercises like body weight squats or sit to stand to relieve any potential stiffness in the knees.)

dx_deadlifts_anteromedial_femoral_condyl.label: Deadlifts - anterior/anteromedial knee pain
dx_deadlifts_anteromedial_femoral_condyl.summary: Assessment for Deadlifts with anteromedial femoral condyle pain
dx_deadlifts_anteromedial_femoral_condyl.explanation: dynamic valgus and/or anterior knee translation; adductors

recommendations.title: Dynamic valgus: slightly turn your toes and knees outward and pretend you're pushing your knee against a wall through...
recommendations.description:
Dynamic valgus: slightly turn your toes and knees outward and pretend you're pushing your knee against a wall throughout the entire movement. Foot width: make sure your feet are placed directly under your hips, if pain persists widen them slightly. 
(If you notice your knees collapse inward, turn your toes and knees slightly outward about 20-230 degrees and think about pushing your knees outward against a wall throughout the entire position.)

recommendations.title: Anterior knee translation/hip hinge: tuck your pelvis before initiating the movement, then sit your butt backwards wh...
recommendations.description:
Anterior knee translation/hip hinge: tuck your pelvis before initiating the movement, then sit your butt backwards while maintgining vertical shin and trace the weight along the front of your shin. (If you notice your knees are moving too far forward past your knees, try to keep your shins vertical/perpendicular to the ground by starting with sitting your butt backwards (as if you were about to sit in a chair) and bringing your trunk forward and chest up.
As you lift the weight, keep your shins vertical by pulling the weight up in a straight line (thin about tracing your shins with the weight).
As you descend, reverse the movement: sit your butt backwards, and continue to trace the front of your shin in a straight line with the weight.)

recommendations.title: ROM: if all of the above has been corrected, perform movement in a pain free range of motion and/or decerase weight
recommendations.description: ROM: if all of the above has been corrected, perform movement in a pain free range of motion and/or decerase weight (If you've tried all the previous corrections and you still notice pain or discomfort, perform the movement in a decreased range of motion. To do this, you can move the weight to a slightly higher surface like blocks.)

dx_deadlifts_anterolateral_femoral_condy.label: Deadlifts - Lateral/anterolateral knee pain
dx_deadlifts_anterolateral_femoral_condy.summary: Assessment for Deadlifts with anterolateral femoral condyle pain
dx_deadlifts_anterolateral_femoral_condy.explanation: ITB friction; varus stress; patellar tracking, increased ER, lateral patellar retinaculum, LCL impairment

recommendations.title: Keep your knees lined up with your second toe; don't let it bow outward or inward, make sure your knees dont move for...
recommendations.description: Keep your knees lined up with your second toe; don't let it bow outward or inward, make sure your knees dont move forward past your toes (keep your shin perpendicular to the ground); (Throughout the movement, try to keep your knees aligned with your 2nd-3rd toes to avoid your knees collapsing inward or falling too far outward. Keep this in mind throughout the entire movement.)

recommendations.title: Anterior knee translation: tuck your pelvis before initiating the movement, then sit your butt backwards while mainta...
recommendations.description:
Anterior knee translation: tuck your pelvis before initiating the movement, then sit your butt backwards while maintaining vertical shins (with slight knee bend) and trace the weight along the front of your shin. (If you notice your knees are moving too far forward past your knees, try to keep your shins vertical/perpendicular to the ground by starting with sitting your butt backwards (as if you were about to sit in a chair) and bringing your trunk forward and chest up.
As you lift the weight, keep your shins vertical by pulling the weight up in a straight line (thin about tracing your shins with the weight).
As you descend, reverse the movement: sit your butt backwards, and continue to trace the front of your shin in a straight line with the weight.)

recommendations.title: Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly
recommendations.description:
Decrease quad activation: focusing on hinging at the hips, lean yout trunk forwrd slightly. (While deadlifting if you notice your trunk is too upright or perpendicular to the ground, bring your trunk flighty forward by hinging at your hips to start the movement. With this modification, be sure to keep your core engaged to avoid increasing load on your back.
Be sure to push through your heels, and squeeze your glutes to lift up the weight.)

recommendations.title: ROM: if all of the above has been corrected, perform movement in a pain free range of motion and/or decrease weight
recommendations.description: ROM: if all of the above has been corrected, perform movement in a pain free range of motion and/or decrease weight (If you've tried all the previous corrections and you still notice pain or discomfort, perform the movement in a decreased range of motion. To do this, you can move the weight to a slightly higher surface like blocks.)

(If you notice your knees collapse inward, turn your toes and knees slightly outward about 20-230 degrees and think about pushing your knees outward against a wall throughout the entire position.)

As you lift the weight, keep your shins vertical by pulling the weight up in a straight line (thin about tracing your shins with the weight).

recommendations.title: Oveall form correction: first tuck your pelvis by engagin your core ans squeezing your gluted. Then, place your feet ...
recommendations.description:
Oveall form correction: first tuck your pelvis by engagin your core ans squeezing your gluted. Then, place your feet durectly beneath your hips (hip width apart). Maintain your core engagement while you sit your butt back, and keep your shins perpendicular to the ground as you grab the weight, maintain this form as you perform the entire movement. If you still have pain after aligning your form, decrease the weight and/or range of motion and perform in a range that is pain free (While in staring position, make sure your core is engaged by tucking your pelvis (bring your belly button towards your spine)
Place your feet hip width part, or slightly wider, 
then sit your butt back, as if you were going to sit in a chair, by hinging at your hips. While doing this make sure you shins stay as vertical as possible as you grab the weight 
As you lift the weight, keep your core engaged, push through your entire foot, and think about tracing the weight in a straight line along the front of your shins)

recommendations.description:
Anterior knee translation/hip hinge: tuck your pelvis before initiating the movement, then sit your butt backwards while maintgining vertical shin and trace the weight along the front of your shin. (If you notice your knees are moving too far forward past your knees, try to keep your shins vertical/perpendicular to the ground as you lower and raise the weight
As you lower the weight, keep your shins vertical by tracing the weight in a straight line down your shin
As you ascend, reverse the movement: continue to trace the front of your shin in a straight line with the weight. )
